287	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
288	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
289	
290	\begin{definition}
291	
292	\leanok
293	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
294	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
296	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
297	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
298	  \[
299	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
300	  \]
301	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
302	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
303	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
304	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
305	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
306	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
307	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
308	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
309	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
310	  canonical compatibility
311	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
312	     \;\cong\;
313	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
314	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
315	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
316	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
317	
318	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
319	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
320	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
321	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
322	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
323	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
324	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
325	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
326	\end{definition}
327	
328	\begin{lemma}
329	
330	\leanok
331	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
332	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
334	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
335	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
336	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
337	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
338	  \[
339	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
340	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
341	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
342	  \]
343	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
344	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
345	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
346	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
347	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
348	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
349	  \[
350	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
351	    \qquad
352	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
353	  \]
354	  the associator
355	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
356	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
357	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
358	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
359	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
360	\end{lemma}
361	
362	\begin{proof}
363	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
364	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
365	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
366	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
367	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
368	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
369	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
370	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
371	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
372	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
373	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
374	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
375	\end{proof}
376	
377	\begin{remark}
378	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
379	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
380	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
381	        lem:whisker_of_W}
382	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
383	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
384	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
385	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
386	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
387	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
388	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
389	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
390	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
391	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
392	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
393	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
394	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
395	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
396	
397	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
398	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
399	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
400	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
401	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
402	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
403	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
404	  fixed-base presheaves in
405	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
406	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
407	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
408	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
409	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
410	  it.
411	
412	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
413	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
414	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
415	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
416	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
417	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
418	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
419	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
420	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
421	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
422	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
423	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
424	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
425	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
426	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
427	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
428	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
429	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
430	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
431	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
432	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
433	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
434	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
435	\end{remark}
436	
437	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
438	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
439	
440	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
441	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
442	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
443	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
444	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
445	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
446	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
447	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
448	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
449	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
450	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
451	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
452	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
453	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
454	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
455	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
456	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
457	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
458	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
459	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
460	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
461	to the off-path route~(e).
462	
463	\begin{lemma}
464	
465	\leanok
466	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
467	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
468	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
469	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
470	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
471	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
472	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
473	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
474	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
475	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
476	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
477	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
478	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
479	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
480	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
481	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
482	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
483	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
484	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
485	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
486	  \[
487	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
488	      \;\;:=\;\;
489	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
490	  \]
491	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
492	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
493	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
494	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
495	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
496	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
497	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
498	\end{lemma}
499	
500	\begin{proof}
501	
502	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
503	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
504	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
505	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
506	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
507	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
508	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
509	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
510	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
511	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
512	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
513	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
514	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
515	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
516	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
517	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
518	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
519	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
520	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
521	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
522	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
523	
524	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
525	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
526	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
527	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
528	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
529	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
530	\end{proof}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}
533	
534	\leanok
535	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
536	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
537	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
538	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
539	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
540	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
541	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
542	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
543	  morphism
544	  \[
545	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
546	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
547	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
548	  \]
549	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
550	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
551	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
552	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
553	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
554	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
555	\end{lemma}
556	
557	\begin{proof}
558	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
559	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
560	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
561	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
562	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
563	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
564	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
565	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
566	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
567	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
568	  isomorphism}
569	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
570	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
571	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
572	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
573	  therefore extension of scalars along
574	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
575	
576	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
577	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
578	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
579	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
580	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
581	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
582	  the abstract pullback functor.
583	
584	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
585	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
586	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
587	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
588	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
589	   \;\cong\;
590	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
591	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
592	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
593	  comparison
594	  \[
595	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
596	      \;\longrightarrow\;
597	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
598	      \;\otimes\;
599	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
600	  \]
601	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
602	
603	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
604	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
605	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
606	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
607	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
608	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
609	  % "\begin{lemma}
610	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
611	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
612	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
613	  % \begin{enumerate}
614	  % \item The functor
615	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
616	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
617	  % \item The functor
618	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
619	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
620	  %  [\dots]
621	  % \end{enumerate}
622	  % \end{lemma}
623	  %  [\dots]
624	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
625	  % of functors, see
626	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
627	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
628	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
629	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
630	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
631	
632	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
633	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
634	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
635	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
636	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
637	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
638	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
639	
640	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
641	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
642	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
643	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
644	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
645	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
646	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
647	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
648	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
649	  \otimes_S N)\).
650	
651	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
652	  \[
653	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
654	  \]
655	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
656	  presheaf-level adjunction
657	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
658	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
659	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
660	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
661	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
662	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
663	  composing the resulting comparison with
664	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
665	
666	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
667	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
668	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
669	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
670	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
671	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
672	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
673	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
674	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
675	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
676	
677	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
678	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
679	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
680	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
681	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
682	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
683	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
684	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
685	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
686	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
687	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
688	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
689	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
690	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
691	\end{proof}

693	\begin{lemma}
694	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
695	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
696	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
697	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
698	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
699	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
700	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
701	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
702	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
703	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
704	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
705	
706	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
707	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
708	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
709	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
710	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
711	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
712	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
713	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
714	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
715	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
716	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
717	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
718	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
719	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
720	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
721	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
722	
723	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
724	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
725	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
726	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
727	  restriction-of-scalars functor
728	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
729	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
730	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
731	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
732	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
733	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
734	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
735	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
736	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
737	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
738	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
739	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
740	
741	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
742	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
743	\end{lemma}

745	\begin{lemma}
746	
747	\leanok
748	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
749	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
750	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
751	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
752	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
753	  product: the canonical map
754	  \[
755	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
756	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
757	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
758	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
759	  \]
760	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
761	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
762	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
763	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
764	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
765	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
766	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
767	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
768	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
769	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
770	\end{lemma}

772	\begin{lemma}
773	
774	[Strong-monoidal upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism (ModuleCat-level H2 core)]
775	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}
776	  \lean{restrictScalars_isIso_μ, restrictScalars_isIso_ε,
777	        restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv,
778	        restrictScalars_isIso_μ_of_bijective,
779	        restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective}
780	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
781	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings and let
782	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom} :
783	  \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,S \to \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) be the associated
784	  restriction-of-scalars functor, equipped with its canonical \emph{lax}-monoidal
785	  structure (the lax tensorator \(\mu\) and lax unit \(\varepsilon\) supplied by
786	  Mathlib). Then:
787	  \begin{enumerate}
788	    \item the lax tensorator
789	      \(\mu_{M, N} : \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e N
790	      \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M \otimes_S N)\) is an isomorphism
791	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));
792	    \item the lax unit
793	      \(\varepsilon : \mathbf{1}_R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(\mathbf{1}_S)\)
794	      is an isomorphism (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon}\));
795	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
796	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
797	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
798	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
799	  \end{enumerate}
800	  The same two invertibility statements hold, more flexibly, under the hypothesis that
801	  the underlying ring homomorphism is merely \emph{bijective} rather than literally of
802	  the form \(e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) for a packaged ring equivalence \(e\)
803	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
804	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
805	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
806	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective
807	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) morphism but is not syntactically a
808	  \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\).
809	\end{lemma}
810	
811	\begin{proof}
812	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
813	  Statements (1) and (2) are the categorical repackaging of
814	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
815	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
816	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
817	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of
818	  \(\mu_{M, N}\) is the additive map \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\) of
819	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}, verified to be two-sided inverse to
820	  the canonical \(R\)-linear \(\mu_{M, N}\); for (2) the unit comparison
821	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
822	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
823	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms
824	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
825	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
826	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring
827	  equivalence'' by ``the underlying ring homomorphism is bijective'' and reconstructing
828	  the inverse ground-ring map from bijectivity; this is what lets the presheaf-level
829	  lift feed in a section-wise-bijective comparison map directly.
830	
831	  These declarations constitute the \emph{ModuleCat-level H2}: strong
832	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism commutes with \(\otimes\). The
833	  one \emph{remaining} H2 step is the bounded, no-Mathlib-gap \emph{presheaf} lift:
834	  assembling \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
835	  section-by-section via \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\), using a
836	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-iso\,\(\Rightarrow\)\,bijective bridge to invoke the
837	  bijective forms above at each section, together with the
838	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) iso-iff-app-iso reflection to lift section-wise
839	  invertibility to the presheaf level; once that lift is in place,
840	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) is monoidal. This presheaf H2 lift is bounded and
841	  has no Mathlib-absent ingredient; the sole genuinely Mathlib-absent piece of
842	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is the presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
843	  (``H1'').
844	\end{proof}

846	\begin{lemma}
847	
848	\leanok
849	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
850	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
851	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
852	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
853	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
854	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
855	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
856	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
857	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
858	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
859	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
860	\end{lemma}
861	
862	\begin{proof}
863	
864	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
865	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
866	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
867	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
868	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
869	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
870	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
871	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
872	\end{proof}

884	\begin{lemma}
885	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
886	
887	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
888	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
889	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
890	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
891	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
892	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
893	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
894	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
895	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
896	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
897	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
898	  left-whiskered morphism
899	  \[
900	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
901	  \]
902	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
903	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
904	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
905	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
906	
907	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
908	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
909	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
910	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
911	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
912	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
913	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
914	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
915	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
916	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
917	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
918	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
919	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
920	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
921	\end{lemma}
922	
923	\begin{proof}
924	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
925	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
926	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
927	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
928	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
929	
930	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
931	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
932	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
933	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
934	  \[
935	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
936	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
937	  \]
938	
939	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
940	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
941	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
942	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
943	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
944	
945	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
946	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
947	  linear maps (Mathlib
948	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
949	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
950	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
951	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
952	
953	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
954	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
955	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
956	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
957	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
958	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
959	\end{proof}

1080	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1081	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1082	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1083	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1084	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1085	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1086	  \[
1087	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1088	  \]
1089	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1090	  characterised on germs by
1091	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1092	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1093	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1094	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1095	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1096	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1097	
1098	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1099	  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
1100	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
1101	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
1102	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1103	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1104	\end{lemma}
1105	
1106	\begin{proof}
1107	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1108	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1109	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1110	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1111	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1112	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1113	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1114	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1115	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1116	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1117	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1118	\end{proof}

1138	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1139	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1140	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1141	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the

1145	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1146	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1147	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1148	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1149	  left-whiskered morphism

1152	  \]

1154	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.

1163	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the

1165	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
1166	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
1167	  primary route already suffices for the group law.

1174	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1175	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1176	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1177	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1178	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1179	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1180	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1181	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).

1183	  \begin{itemize}

1185	      gives a canonical isomorphism

1188	      \]

1190	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)

1287	\begin{lemma}
1288	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1289	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1290	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1291	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1292	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1293	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1294	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1295	  \(J.W\):
1296	  \[
1297	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1298	    \qquad
1299	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1300	  \]
1301	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1302	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1303	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1304	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1305	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1306	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1307	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1308	\end{lemma}
1309	
1310	\begin{proof}
1311	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1312	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1313	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1314	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1315	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1316	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1317	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1318	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1319	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1320	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1321	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1322	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1323	\end{proof}
1324	
1325	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1326	the route~(e) target: a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on all of
1327	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\). It is not
1328	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1329	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, assembled by hand
1330	rather than off a coherent monoidal category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)), and the
1331	associator is now built directly by
1332	gluing (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This
1333	block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized.
1334	
1335	\begin{lemma}
1336	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1337	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1338	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1339	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1340	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1341	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1342	  instance
1343	  \[
1344	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1345	  \]
1346	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1347	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1348	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1349	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1350	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1351	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1352	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1353	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1354	  category
1355	  \[
1356	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1357	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1358	  \]
1359	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1360	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1361	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1362	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1363	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1364	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1365	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1366	  composites.
1367	
1368	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it depends on the sole open obligation
1369	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1370	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1371	\end{lemma}
1372	
1373	\begin{proof}
1374	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1375	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1376	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1377	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1378	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1379	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1380	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1381	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1382	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1383	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1384	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1385	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1386	  at any point.
1387	\end{proof}

1389	\begin{lemma}
1390	
1391	\leanok
1392	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1393	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1396	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1397	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1398	  \[
1399	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1400	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1401	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1402	  \]
1403	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1404	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1405	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1406	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1407	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1408	  ever required.
1409	
1410	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1411	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1412	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1413	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1414	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1415	\end{lemma}
1416	
1417	\begin{proof}
1418	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1419	  \textbf{Status (route mismatch, deferred).} The \emph{current} realization of this
1420	  associator uses the route-(d) whiskering composite
1421	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} and the sheafification
1422	  bridge \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}); through
1423	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- whose left-whiskering component is
1424	  itself unproved --- it transitively depends on an open proof obligation and is
1425	  therefore \emph{not} axiom-clean. The gluing route described below is the
1426	  \emph{intended} obligation-free realization, but discharging it requires
1427	  \emph{morphism-level descent for \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)}: gluing a family of
1428	  local isomorphisms, agreeing on overlaps, into a single global isomorphism of
1429	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. That is the same descent-infrastructure family
1430	  the \(\otimes\)-inverse needs (\cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}); accordingly, the
1431	  associator re-route \emph{and} the deletion of the vestigial whiskering/stalk
1432	  apparatus are deferred jointly with the dual block, and are not available with the
1433	  current infrastructure. The gluing description below records the target realization.
1434	
1435	  The isomorphism is built by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through the
1436	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, with no
1437	  recourse to any whiskering or stalk apparatus. Fix a
1438	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1439	  composite
1440	  \[
1441	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1442	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1443	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1444	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1445	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1446	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1447	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1448	  \]
1449	  in which:
1450	  \begin{itemize}
1451	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1452	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1453	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1454	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1455	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1456	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1457	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1458	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1459	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1460	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1461	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1462	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1463	  \end{itemize}
1464	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1465	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1466	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1467	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1468	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1469	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1470	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1471	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1472	  composites agree on the overlap.
1473	
1474	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1475	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1476	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1477	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1478	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1479	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1480	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1481	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1482	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1483	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1484	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1485	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1486	
1487	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1488	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1489	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1490	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1491	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1492	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1493	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1494	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1495	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1496	  specialisation can replace it. The vestigial
1497	  whiskering / \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus
1498	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}) plays
1499	  no part in this proof and is superseded. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1500	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1501	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1502	\end{proof}
1503	
1504	\begin{lemma}
1505	[Left and right unitors for $\otimes_X$]
1506	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1507	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor, AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor}
1508	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1509	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) an
1510	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. With
1511	  \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) the
1512	  structure-sheaf unit object, there are natural isomorphisms
1513	  \[
1514	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M,
1515	    \qquad
1516	    M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M .
1517	  \]
1518	\end{lemma}
1519	
1520	\begin{proof}
1521	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1522	  The presheaf monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) supplies
1523	  the left and right unitors \(\lambda, \rho\) at the presheaf level. Their
1524	  sheafifications \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\lambda)\),
1525	  \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\rho)\), composed with the sheafification
1526	  counit identifying the sheafified structure-sheaf presheaf with
1527	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the step already used in the structure-sheaf-unit
1528	  comparison \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) in the substrate file), give the
1529	  two isomorphisms. This route is the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern and uses no
1530	  abstract pullback.
1531	\end{proof}

1556	\begin{lemma}
1557	
1558	\leanok
1559	[Inverse of an invertible module]
1560	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
1561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
1562	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
1563	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1564	  % NOTE: ON the critical path under the PRIMARY locally-trivial group law (it
1565	  % supplies inverses to lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, in whose uses it sits).
1566	  % Only under the route-(e) fallback is it dispensable: there the group law uses
1567	  % the tensor-invertibility predicate IsInvertible (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible),
1568	  % which carries the inverse existentially, so no separate ``a tensor inverse
1569	  % exists'' lemma is required.
1570	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
1571	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2: the dual of an
1572	  % invertible is invertible and the contraction map is an isomorphism) +
1573	  % Definition~definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
1574	  % iso-classes of invertibles)
1575	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213 and L4350--L4357).
1576	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1577	  % "\begin{lemma}
1578	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
1579	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1580	  % ...
1581	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
1582	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
1583	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1584	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
1585	  % is an isomorphism.
1586	  % ...
1587	  % \begin{definition}
1588	  % \label{definition-pic}
1589	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1590	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
1591	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
1592	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
1593	  % corresponding to tensor product.
1594	  % \end{definition}"
1595	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1596	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction
1597	  is an isomorphism) and Definition~\texttt{definition-pic} (the Picard group is
1598	  the abelian group of iso-classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules under
1599	  tensor product).}
1600	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
1601	  bundle. The dual
1602	  \[
1603	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1604	  \]
1605	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
1606	  isomorphism
1607	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
1608	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
1609	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
1610	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1611	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1612	\end{lemma}
1613	
1614	\begin{proof}
1615	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1616	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
1617	  \textbf{Infrastructure-blocked.} This statement is \emph{not} realizable with the
1618	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module infrastructure presently available: its construction
1619	  depends on a \emph{sheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules}
1620	  \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\), an object that does not yet
1621	  exist at the presheaf, sheaf, or general-categorical level (there is no
1622	  monoidal-closed / internal-hom / dual structure on
1623	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) or \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), and no object-level
1624	  descent assembling a global \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module from local pieces). The dual
1625	  object \(L^{-1}\) therefore cannot even be \emph{named} until that infrastructure
1626	  is built. The construction of the required internal hom, its dual, and its
1627	  evaluation is decomposed into individually-formalizable sub-steps in
1628	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} through
1629	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}); the present lemma is realized once those land,
1630	  and its Lean body is a placeholder until then.
1631	
1632	  We record the intended mathematical route, all three steps of which are correct
1633	  modulo the missing object. \emph{The route is as follows.} Take
1634	  \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) to be the
1635	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, and let
1636	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) be the evaluation of
1637	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}.
1638	
1639	  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
1640	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
1641	  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
1642	  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
1643	  \[
1644	    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1645	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1646	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
1647	  \]
1648	  the last identification being the evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism for the dual of a
1649	  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
1650	  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
1651	
1652	  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
1653	  morphism
1654	  \[
1655	    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
1656	      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1657	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
1658	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
1659	  \]
1660	  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
1661	  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1662	  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
1663	  \[
1664	    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
1665	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
1666	  \]
1667	  using the trivialisations of Step~1. Under these identifications \(\varepsilon_L|_U\)
1668	  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
1669	  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
1670	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
1671	  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.
1672	
1673	  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
1674	  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
1675	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1676	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
1677	  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
1678	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
1679	  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
1680	  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
1681	  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
1682	  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).
1683	
1684	  Consequently, \emph{once \(L^{-1}\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) are available from
1685	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}}, every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor
1686	  inverse \(L^{-1}\) under \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of
1687	  line bundles is an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1688	  The local-triviality of \(L^{-1}\) (Step~1) is \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} and
1689	  the local-iso\(\Rightarrow\)global-iso passage (Step~3) is the already-closed
1690	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the single non-available input is the dual object
1691	  and its evaluation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
1692	\end{proof}
1693	
1694	\begin{lemma}
1695	
1696	\leanok
1697	[Closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial carrier
1698	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
1699	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
1701	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1702	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1703	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. The carrier is the
1704	  \emph{locally-trivial} subtype
1705	  \[
1706	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1707	    \;\;=\;\;
1708	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
1709	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}
1710	  \]
1711	  introduced in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. The bifunctor
1712	  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
1713	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to this subtype:
1714	  if \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M'\) then
1715	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\). Hence \(\otimes\)
1716	  closes on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\), supplying the binary
1717	  operation the relative group law multiplies with.
1718	\end{lemma}
1719	
1720	\begin{proof}
1721	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1722	  Closure is exactly
1723	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}: the tensor product of two
1724	  locally-trivial rank-one modules is again locally trivial of rank one
1725	  (\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_isLocallyTrivial}\)). The Lean construction
1726	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjOnProduct}\) applies this directly to a pair of objects of
1727	  the \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) subtype \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\),
1728	  returning the tensor with its locally-trivial witness; no
1729	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate and no coherence isomorphism enters this
1730	  closure statement. (The \(\otimes\)-invertibility carrier
1731	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} is a
1732	  separate predicate, connected to \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) only by an
1733	  off-critical-path equivalence; the units-group packaging is
1734	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, not this lemma.)
1735	\end{proof}
1736	
1737	\begin{lemma}
1738	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1739	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1740	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1741	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1742	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1743	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1744	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1745	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1746	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1747	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1748	  functor
1749	  \[
1750	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1751	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1752	  \]
1753	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1754	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1755	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1756	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1757	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1758	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1759	  canonical isomorphism
1760	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1761	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1762	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1763	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1764	\end{lemma}
1765	
1766	\begin{proof}
1767	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1768	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1769	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1770	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1771	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1772	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1773	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1774	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1775	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1776	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1777	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1778	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1779	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1780	  same cover.
1781	\end{proof}

1783	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1784	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1785	
1786	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1787	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1788	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1789	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1790	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1791	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1792	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
1793	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
1794	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
1795	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
1796	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
1797	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
1798	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
1799	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1918	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules

1922	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1923	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1924	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1925	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1926	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1927	  01CR).
1928	
1929	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1930	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1931	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1932	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1933	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1934	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1935	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1936	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1937	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1938	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1939	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1940	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1941	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1942	
1943	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1944	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1945	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1946	  \[
1947	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1948	    \quad
1949	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1950	    \quad
1951	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1952	    \quad
1953	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1954	  \]
1955	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1956	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1957	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1958	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1959	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1960	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1961	
1962	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1963	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1964	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1965	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1966	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1967	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1968	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1969	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1970	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1971	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1972	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1973	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1974	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1975	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1976	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1977	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1978	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1979	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1980	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1981	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1982	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1983	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1984	  pinned commit.
1985	
1986	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1987	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1988	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1989	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1990	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1991	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1992	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1993	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1994	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1995	
1996	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1997	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1998	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1999	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
2000	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
2001	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
2002	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
2003	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
2004	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
2005	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
2006	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
2007	  already supplies.
2008	\end{lemma}
